summary: This is a brief news roundup post from Joel Spolsky covering several short items. He shares positive reviews of Fog Creek Copilot's remote support software, announces a keynote at EclipseCon 2006, and promotes an early Ajax development book. The post highlights Fog Creek's growing product line and Joel's speaking profile, while noting the team's increasing interest in Ajax web development techniques.
key_points: Fog Creek Copilot received praise for its simplicity and ability to work through NAT routers without hassle
Joel was gaining prominence as a speaker, landing a keynote at EclipseCon 2006
Ajax was emerging as a key web development technique, enabling rich client-like experiences in the browser
Applications like Gmail, Google Maps, and Flickr were early exemplars of Ajax's potential
money_quotes: It does exactly what it says it does – with gusto!
I've used remote support software before but it's usually hassle getting it through your average NAT router. The copilot service just worked when I tried it.
Ajax applications are highly dynamic web applications that are nearly as feature-rich as their thick client brethren.
All those cool features in GMail, Google Maps, Flickr, and Oddpost that make them feel much more responsive than typical web applications are thanks to Ajax techniques.
key_insight: A lightweight news post capturing a moment when Ajax was transforming web development and Fog Creek was building products focused on removing friction from common tasks like remote support.
